questions.question: A firm's customers have inverse demand P(Q)=100-2Q. The firm has constant marginal cost MC=$20 and chooses Q to maximize total surplus, not profit. What quantity is efficient, and what is the interpretation?

choices.A: Q=20; marginal willingness to pay equals marginal cost.
choices.B: Q=40; marginal willingness to pay equals marginal cost.
choices.C: Q=25; marginal revenue equals marginal cost.

questions.explanation: Efficiency requires inverse demand, or marginal WTP, equal MC: 100-2Q=20, so Q=40. A uses monopoly MR=100-4Q=20. C is another MR-related calculation error. D is the zero-price quantity, not efficient with positive MC.

questions.question: Market demand is Q=60-3P. What is inverse demand, and what is marginal willingness to pay for the 30th unit?

choices.A: P=20-Q/3; marginal willingness to pay for the 30th unit is $10.
choices.B: P=60-3Q; marginal willingness to pay for the 30th unit is -$30.
choices.C: P=180-3Q; marginal willingness to pay for the 30th unit is $90.
choices.D: P=20-3Q; marginal willingness to pay for the 30th unit is -$70.

questions.explanation: Solve Q=60-3P for P: P=20-Q/3. At Q=30, P=10, the marginal WTP for the last unit. B fails to invert. C multiplies by 3 incorrectly. D keeps the wrong slope.

questions.question: Inverse demand for online courses shifts from P=80-4Q to P=100-4Q after employer reimbursement becomes available. At Q=10, what happens to marginal willingness to pay and why?

choices.A: It rises from $40 to $60 because reimbursement raises value at every quantity.
choices.B: It falls from $40 to $20 because higher demand lowers price.
choices.C: It stays $40 because the slope is unchanged.
choices.D: It rises from $10 to $25 because quantity is divided by the slope.

questions.explanation: At Q=10, old MWTP is 80-40=40 and new MWTP is 100-40=60. The upward intercept shift raises marginal WTP at each quantity. B confuses movement along demand with demand shift. C ignores intercept change. D misuses the slope.

questions.question: A linear inverse demand curve changes from P=50-Q to P=50-2Q. At Q=15, what happens to marginal willingness to pay, and what is the mechanism?

choices.A: It rises from $35 to $20 because demand becomes steeper.
choices.B: It falls from $35 to $20 because marginal willingness to pay declines faster with quantity.
choices.C: It stays $35 because the intercept is unchanged.
choices.D: It falls from $50 to $35 because the choke price changes.

questions.explanation: Old MWTP at 15 is 50-15=35. New MWTP is 50-30=20. A says rises despite lower value. C ignores the slope change. D uses the intercept and a wrong choke-price statement.

questions.question: On a graph with price on the vertical axis and quantity on the horizontal axis, what does the height of the inverse demand curve at Q=25 represent?

choices.A: Total willingness to pay for all 25 units.
choices.C: Marginal willingness to pay for the 25th unit.

questions.explanation: Inverse demand height at a quantity is the marginal WTP for the last unit. A is the area under demand. B is area above price. D is price times quantity.

questions.question: For inverse demand P=40-0.5Q, the marginal revenue curve has the same vertical intercept and twice the slope in absolute value. Which verbal graph statement is correct?

choices.A: MR starts at $40 and crosses the quantity axis at Q=40, while demand crosses at Q=80.
choices.B: MR starts at $80 and crosses the quantity axis at Q=40.
choices.C: MR is identical to inverse demand because both show willingness to pay.
choices.D: MR lies above inverse demand because selling more units raises price.

questions.explanation: TR=(40-0.5Q)Q=40Q-0.5Q^2, so MR=40-Q. MR has intercept 40 and zero at Q=40; demand zero is Q=80. B doubles the intercept. C confuses price with marginal revenue. D gets the relationship backward for downward-sloping demand.

questions.question: Why is inverse demand often interpreted as marginal willingness to pay?

choices.A: Because it tells the maximum price consumers will pay for the last unit of a given quantity.
choices.B: Because it tells the minimum cost firms need to produce each unit.
choices.C: Because it equals total revenue at each quantity.

questions.explanation: Inverse demand maps quantity to the maximum price consumers are willing to pay for the marginal unit. B describes marginal cost. C confuses price with revenue. D is unrelated.

questions.question: A city wants to decide the efficient number of public pool admissions when marginal cost is $5 and inverse demand for admissions is P=25-0.1Q. Which condition should it apply?

choices.A: Set total revenue equal to total cost.
choices.B: Set inverse demand equal to marginal cost, 25-0.1Q=5.
choices.C: Set marginal revenue equal to zero.
choices.D: Set quantity equal to the price intercept.

questions.explanation: For efficient quantity, marginal WTP equals marginal cost. A is a break-even condition. C is revenue maximization. D confuses intercept with optimal quantity.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes demand from inverse demand?

choices.A: Demand gives Q as a function of P; inverse demand gives P as a function of Q.
choices.B: Demand applies to buyers; inverse demand applies only to sellers.
choices.C: Demand measures total surplus; inverse demand measures producer surplus.
choices.D: Demand is always nonlinear; inverse demand is always linear.

questions.explanation: Ordinary demand is Q(P); inverse demand is P(Q). B, C, and D are false distinctions.

questions.question: Inverse demand for kayak rentals is P=60-2Q. Market price is $20. What is consumer surplus?
questions.explanation: Set 60-2Q=20, so Q=20. CS=0.5×20×(60-20)=$400. B doubles the triangle. C uses half the correct quantity. D uses an incorrect height of 60.
